payload.name: decision
payload.description: Record a design decision as evidence: choice, why (the reasoning), and what it was chosen instead of. Arg: 'choice :: why :: instead-of'.
payload.implementation:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# decision — record a design decision as evidence: the choice, the WHY (the
# reasoning), and what it was chosen instead of. "A seed carries the work, not
# just the conclusion." Arg format: "choice :: why :: instead-of".
import sys, json
parts = [p.strip() for p in " ".join(sys.argv[1:]).split("::")]
print(json.dumps({"name": "decision.made", "payload": {
    "choice":   parts[0] if len(parts) > 0 else "",
    "why":      parts[1] if len(parts) > 1 else "",
    "rejected": parts[2] if len(parts) > 2 else "",
}}))

payload.name: brief
payload.description: The project brief: every decision.made and its reasoning, oldest first.

payload.choice: The kernel knows almost nothing; capabilities are LLM-compiled from seeds
payload.why: Keep the trusted core tiny and auditable — everything that can change lives in the seed layer, re-grown per receiver against its own garden. This is ks's whole reason to exist: the minimal runnable form of the Knowledge Seed Protocol.
payload.rejected: A large hand-coded kernel with built-in features

payload.choice: Sandbox the compile-time bash tool with a fail-closed allowlist
payload.why: The real threat is prompt-injected seed content steering the read tool; an allowlist denies the unknown by default instead of chasing a denylist's forgotten gaps. (We later found and closed write-escapes in sort/uniq/find that a denylist missed.)

payload.choice: The strange loop carries SPECS, not code — the LLM is always the compiler
payload.why: Every binary is authored for THIS receiver against its own event vocabulary, so receiver-controlled adaptation is never skipped. The moment a seed ships a ready binary, adaptation dies and the attack surface goes unbounded.
payload.rejected: Letting a command emit script.compiled to install exact code / shipped binaries

payload.choice: Exact-code reuse exists only as rollback, and only the kernel performs it
payload.why: Reinstalling the receiver's OWN prior, already-run output adds no attack surface and no drift; importing foreign bytes would break both adaptation and trust. Restore is install-from-receipt, the same privilege as compile.
payload.rejected: A code-carrying strange loop, byte-identical replicators / quine seeds

payload.choice: script.compiled is signed with a per-home secret; install verifies the signature
payload.why: Provenance becomes intrinsic to the receipt instead of enforced by dropping the event at every ingress (provenance by vigilance). Only kernel-authored code installs, and signatures are meaningful only on the receiver that produced them — so you still can't import another node's binaries, only its declarations.
payload.rejected: Reserving the event by ingress-filtering, or moving compiles to a separate kernel build log

payload.choice: Rollback is a seed: a data-only restore.requested event the kernel acts on
payload.why: Separate who PERFORMS the install (the kernel, privileged) from who TRIGGERS it (anyone — the trigger carries only a name+seq, never code). So restore is an ordinary capability, reachable by CLI, brain, or any seed, with no special verb or tool.
payload.rejected: A bespoke kernel 'restore' verb plus a special brain restore-tool

payload.choice: A seed carries precise code as a reference implementation, not a shipped binary
payload.why: The compiler verifies it against the pipe contract and adapts it to the local garden — near-identical power to handing over code, but coherent with receiver adaptation and adding zero foreign-code attack surface.
payload.rejected: Shipping ready-to-run scripts inside the seed

payload.choice: The brain is an OpenAI-compatible localhost endpoint; any agent can be it
payload.why: Decouple intelligence from the kernel — a human, or Claude over a mailbox bridge, can drive the whole loop — and let us swap the LLM freely. The bet: smaller, far faster models could experiment wildly inside this substrate.
payload.rejected: Hardwiring one LLM client into the kernel

payload.choice: think accepts turn-based JSON input, not just a single string
payload.why: Lets a chat capability hand the brain native turns plus its own identity as a leading system turn, with zero kernel knowledge of chat. Minimal change, in spirit — the `self think "..."` string path is untouched.
payload.rejected: Making chat dump its HTML page for the brain to re-read

payload.choice: Memory is three read-depths of one log; compaction collapses the view, not the truth
payload.why: Working memory is the un-folded tail of turns; situational memory is a shell over the garden; deep memory is the full event log. Folding writes a summary event and never deletes — so compaction is reversible, inspectable, and fully recoverable, not lossy amnesia.
payload.rejected: A context window that compacts lossily and hopes it went okay

payload.choice: Identity is a projection: a self.identity event rendered to a page, prepended by chat
payload.why: The system prompt becomes inspectable and self-editable like everything else — the same self seen through a different prism — rather than a constant buried in the binary. Self can revise who it is by appending an event, and you can read the diff.

payload.choice: Record the project's own design decisions as a knowledge seed (this brief)
payload.why: ks should remember WHY it is the way it is, projected for whoever — human or agent — opens it next. The why IS the evidence chain; in KSP's terms the seed carries the work, not just the conclusion, and replays anywhere it's grown.
payload.rejected: Letting the rationale live only in commit messages and a chat transcript that compacts away
